citation.title: Onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression in membrane distillation

content: A B S T R A C T In this study, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is used to characterize the onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression in real time through commercial polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane distillation (MD) membranes. Results showed that higher salinities, elevated transmembrane hydraulic pressures, and larger membrane pore sizes are associated with earlier onset, higher rates, and/or greater depths of wetting front progression – but, notably, in the absence of pore breakthrough. It was also shown that wetting is not an on/off switch; instead, membrane wetting resistance acts as a frictional force that can slow down or stop wetting front progression at different depths in the membrane. PTFE MD membranes were observed to often operate in a deeply wetted, but stable, transition state with normalized impedance values of ~0.6 to ~0.1, indicating middepth wetting, where the wetting front progresses into the bulk of the membrane and holds its position, or deep wetting, where the wetting front holds its position near the distillate-side surface of the membrane without pore breakthrough. Normalized impedance values as low as 0.04 held constant throughout a 14-day experiment in which pore breakthrough did not occur.

These new impedance results strongly support the hypothesis that during MD, for saline feed solutions and under elevated transmembrane pressures – and especially for larger membrane pore sizes – the distillate-side surface can act as a final barrier to pore breakthrough.

citation.title: Onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression in membrane distillation

content: 1. Introduction Membrane distillation (MD) is a thermally driven desalination pro­cess that is competitive for recovering water from challenging, highsalinity streams [1–7]. In MD, an air gap within the pores of a hydro­phobic membrane separates a warmer feed stream from a cooler distil­late stream. Mass transfer of water vapor across the membrane is driven by the vapor pressure difference across the air gap [8,9]. Since the vapor pressure driving force is only slightly reduced at high salinity, MD can be more suitable for high salinity or high recovery applications when compared to conventional reverse osmosis (RO) [8,10]. While other emerging pressure-driven membrane processes such as high-pressure reverse osmosis, osmotically assisted reverse osmosis, and low-salt-rejection reverse osmosis are also being considered for high-salinity and/or high-recovery applications, these processes are complicated by limited availability of membranes [11,12] and severe membrane fouling and scaling [13,14]. In addition, these processes are relatively immature when compared with MD. Using MD, high recovery and high solute rejection can be achieved at lower pressures and with lower fouling rates, as long as membrane integrity is maintained.

citation.title: Onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression in membrane distillation

content:
Under ideal operating conditions, hydrophobic MD membranes are expected to maintain an air gap separating the liquid feed and distillate streams. The wetting front (i.e., the liquid-vapor interface between the feed solution and the air gap within membrane pores) is expected to be at or near the feed-side surface of the membrane. Wetting front pro­gression and pore breakthrough (i.e., progression of the liquid feed stream through the entire depth of the membrane, where it would mix with the liquid distillate stream) are not expected to occur. However, under real operating conditions, wetting front progression and pore breakthrough have frequently been observed (e.g., Refs. [3,8,15–17]). Wetting is often associated with low surface tension of the feed solution, with organic fouling, and with salt crystallization (scaling) on the membrane surface [9,15,18,19]. Scaling-induced wetting is caused by formation and growth of salt crystals on the membrane surface at pore entrances or on pore walls, due to concentration polarization [20,21]. Salt crystals effectively modify the interfacial energy and hydropho­bicity of the membrane pore walls/fibrils and make wetting front pro­gression thermodynamically feasible [9,20,22,23]. Scaling-induced wetting has typically been discussed for less-soluble salt solutions, such as CaSO4, (e.g., Refs. [9,24]), which are associated with greater

However, scaling-induced wetting by highly soluble salts, including NaCl, was also observed recently by Shi et al. [20]. NaCl feed solutions were observed to result in pore break­through for both polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and polyvinylidene fluoride membranes; wetting was attributed to scaling within pores. Shi et al. [20] suggested that highly-soluble NaCl crystals within pores could readily redissolve (due to reduced local solute concentration and vapor flux, reducing internal concentration polarization) and then recrystallize (due to increased local vapor flux and internal concentration polariza­tion), facilitating further scaling and wetting front progression that eventually resulted in pore breakthrough. This was termed a “self-­limiting” wetting effect [20]. To quantify the resistance of MD membranes to wetting, researchers have conventionally used measured or theoretical liquid entry pressure (LEP). When transmembrane hydraulic pressure (TMP) values exceed the LEP, wetting front progression leading to pore breakthrough is ex­pected to occur.

citation.title: Onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression in membrane distillation

content: LEP). When transmembrane hydraulic pressure (TMP) values exceed the LEP, wetting front progression leading to pore breakthrough is ex­pected to occur. To determine theoretical LEP (LEPth), researchers have most frequently used the Young-Laplace model for cylindrical pores [25]: LEPth = -4BγLV cos θdp where B is the pore geometry factor, γLV is the liquid-vapor surface tension, θ is the intrinsic contact angle of the membrane material, and dp is the pore diameter. While LEP can predict pressure-driven pore breakthrough, LEP may not predict scaling-induced pore breakthrough well [3,26,27]. The effects of scaling on the effective contact angle and pore size are not well-characterized because the rates of scaling may vary over the membrane surface. Thus, experimental measurements of wetting front progression and pore breakthrough are necessary to characterize membrane wetting resistance when scaling may occur. Conventionally, distillate conductivity is measured during MD ex­periments to ensure that pore breakthrough has not occurred. Distillate conductivity can also be used to calculate solute rejection [3,26,28]. Because pore breakthrough results in solute flux, when breakthrough occurs, distillate conductivity increases and solute rejection decreases.

content: ,26,28]. Because pore breakthrough results in solute flux, when breakthrough occurs, distillate conductivity increases and solute rejection decreases. Distillate conductivity can also increase due to experimental artifacts; however, as shown in previous work from our group (i.e., McGaughey and Childress [3]), experimental artifacts can be identified based on the mode of increase of distillate conductivity. Therefore, increasing distil­late conductivity can indicate when pore breakthrough occurs. Notably, prior studies using distillate conductivity monitoring to indicate pore breakthrough have demonstrated that pore breakthrough can occur under moderate operating conditions that are not expected to exceed membrane wetting resistance limits. In 2022, McGaughey and Childress [3] observed pore breakthrough when operating with feed solutions well below saturation and at TMP well below the LEP. In 2023, Hardikar et al. [27] investigated MD using municipal wastewater as feed solution and also found that pore breakthrough occurred for feed solutions well below saturation, and at TMP well below the LEP. The authors suggested that pore deformation due to spacers can result in pore breakthrough at TMP lower than the LEP.

Additionally, pore breakthrough may be overlooked in bench-scale studies even when it would be observed in larger-scale systems, because liquid flow rates through wetted pores are expected to be greater for larger membrane areas and higher TMP, as often occur in larger-scale systems [27]. This is especially likely if distillate conductivity is the only indicator being used to detect pore breakthrough, as distillate conductivity monitoring cannot be used to understand wetting front progression prior to pore breakthrough. To characterize the depth of wetting front progression prior to pore breakthrough during MD, researchers have developed several methods, namely optical in-situ wetting detection (e.g., Ref. [29]), current mea­surement (e.g., Ref. [30]), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (e.g., Refs. [31–34]). In 2022, Zhang et al. [29] developed optical in-situ wetting detection, in which the intensity of light transmitted through the membrane is used to indicate wetting front progression. Notably, light transmission can be affected by membrane scaling/foul­ing; therefore, this method may not be applicable to solution chemistries with scaling/fouling potential or containing opaque particles [29,35]. Further, membrane materials may affect applicability [35]. In 2017, Ahmed et al.

content: ith scaling/fouling potential or containing opaque particles [29,35]. Further, membrane materials may affect applicability [35]. In 2017, Ahmed et al. [30] developed a method to use electrical current mea­surement to indicate wetting depth using a conductive membrane. However, this measurement can only be used for conductive mem­branes, which are not common for MD. Also in 2017, Chen et al. [32] developed a real-time method to use EIS to determine the average depth of the wetting front inside non-conductive, polymeric MD membranes [32,33]. They used EIS to measure the impedance across a hydrophobic MD membrane saturated with water vapor, which can be modeled as a circuit of a resistor and a capacitor in parallel. The measured impedance is the sum of the resis­tance of the membrane material and the capacitive reactance of the air gap [32]. Because initial impedance values can be slightly different depending on membranes, feed solutions, and/or experimental condi­tions, past studies have used normalized impedance values instead of raw impedance values [31,32]. A normalized impedance value of one indicates that the wetting front is at the feed-side surface of the mem­brane. As the wetting front progresses deeper into the membrane and the air gap decreases, the capacitance increases because the reduced air gap results in greater overall permittivity. Capacitance is inversely propor­tional to capacitive reactance, so as capacitance increases, capacitive reactance decreases.

citation.title: Onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression in membrane distillation

content: ter overall permittivity. Capacitance is inversely propor­tional to capacitive reactance, so as capacitance increases, capacitive reactance decreases. Therefore, the impedance across the membrane decreases as the wetting front progresses into the membrane pores. When the air gap closes (i.e., pore breakthrough occurs), normalized impedance will approach some minimum value close to zero that cor­responds to the normalized impedance across the wetted membrane. Due to differences in pore diameters, the wetting front may progress more in some pores and less in others. The measured impedance reflects the average depth of wetting front progression in the membrane and cannot differentiate between wetting front progression in individual pores. Also, measured impedance cannot differentiate between break­through of one pore or of many pores because the electrical circuit is complete in both cases and the normalized impedance will be the same minimum value [32]. Additionally, because the minimum value of normalized impedance that corresponds to pore breakthrough is un­known and may differ between different membranes, normalized impedance alone is insufficient to determine if pore breakthrough has occurred. To determine pore breakthrough as well as the onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression, measuring distillate conductivity alongside real-time impedance is necessary.

content: ough as well as the onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression, measuring distillate conductivity alongside real-time impedance is necessary. By monitoring distillate conductivity and real-time impedance together, wetting front progres­sion and pore breakthrough can be characterized even at the bench scale, when pore flow rates are low. Recently, Wang et al. [31,36] used EIS to develop mechanistic un­derstanding and models that describe surfactant-induced pore wetting in MD. In the classic LEP models, if the TMP exceeds the LEP, the wetting front will progress from the pore entrance through the pore depth until pore breakthrough occurs, without any observable transition state (i.e., state in which the wetting front is located within the pore depth, as defined by Wang et al. [31] and Horseman et al. [9]). As expected, no transition state has been reported when the TMP is increased above the LEP, or under typical operating TMP values when the surface tension of the feed solution is reduced such that the LEP is less than the TMP by the addition of alcohols to the feed solution [9]. An observable, but unstable transition state was reported by Wang et al. [31] when surfactants are present in the feed solution. Wetting front progression was observed to occur continuously, but gradually [9,31,36].

content: et al. [31] when surfactants are present in the feed solution. Wetting front progression was observed to occur continuously, but gradually [9,31,36]. Surfactants reduce the surface tension of the feed solution, but also adsorb onto pore walls, which decreases the local surfactant concentration and increases the local surface tension of the feed solution. This results in gradual wetting front progression that depends on the bulk surfactant concentration and adsorption rates [31,36]. Given that the adsorption capacity of the pore

content: S.G. Joshi et al. Journal of Membrane Science 713 (2025) 123253 wall is limited and assuming that surfactant concentrations exceed that capacity (as is expected in real feed solutions), the reduced surface tension of the feed solution (such that the LEP is less than the TMP) would allow the wetting front to continuously progress through the membrane with no metastable or stable transition states occurring [9,31,36]. According to this current understanding of wetting, the wetting front is expected to be at or near the feed-side surface of the membrane under non-wetting conditions. Under wetting conditions, wetting front pro­gression will occur continuously and result in pore breakthrough. While transition states may be observable, they are not metastable or stable. On the contrary, several studies have implicitly suggested that meta­stable or stable transition states – in which the wetting front holds its position at some depth within the membrane pores over long operating times – can occur. In 2019 and 2020, Wang et al. [37] and Li et al. [38] used EIS and fluorescence microscopy to characterize wetting front progression in MD membranes with surfactant-containing feed solu­tions. Compared to omniphobic membranes, for which no wetting front progression was observed, for hydrophobic quartz fiber and PVDF membranes, wetting front progression did occur.

content: membranes, for which no wetting front progression was observed, for hydrophobic quartz fiber and PVDF membranes, wetting front progression did occur. It was observed that wetting front progression was limited to the top ~10–30 μm of the membrane pores [37,38], which implies that a metastable or stable transition state occurred in which the wetting front held its position within the membrane pores. Furthermore, for operating conditions resulting in surface hydrophilization or scaling of MD membranes, metastable or stable transition states may exist in which the wetting front progresses well beyond the pore entrance but holds its position at some depth within the membrane pores. In early work, McGaughey et al. [6] observed that feed-side membrane surface contact angle and roughness decreased after long-term use (i.e., up to 100 days) in MD. However, despite feed-side surface hydrophilization, pore breakthrough was not observed [6]. Although EIS was not used, these results suggest that wetting fronts progress to some depth within membrane pores, but slow down or stop at different depths, where wetting resistance is maintained [6]. McGaughey et al. [6] hypothesized that the hydro­phobic internal and/or distillate-side surface of the membrane act as final barriers to wetting front progression. This contradicts current un­derstanding of wetting front progression (i.e., that metastable or stable transition states do not exist).

However, prior studies have not investi­gated the occurrence or stability of transitional wetting states. More­over, although prior studies using EIS have well-characterized wetting front progression and pore breakthrough with surfactant-containing feed solutions (e.g., Refs. [32,33,36,39,40]), EIS has not been used to characterize wetting front progression for saline feed solutions in which scaling may occur, or under elevated TMP below the LEP. Also, notably, membrane compaction can affect impedance inde­pendent of wetting front progression but has not been considered in prior studies that use EIS to characterize wetting front progression in MD membranes. In recent work, Ding et al. [4] developed a novel method to integrate EIS with dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) to characterize membrane compaction in real-time under low applied pressures. At low TMP (<10 kPa), normalized impedance decreased across commercial, microporous PTFE MD membranes, primarily due to instant compaction [4]. Therefore, decreases in normalized impedance associated with instant compaction must be subtracted out when evaluating impedance data.

content: compaction [4]. Therefore, decreases in normalized impedance associated with instant compaction must be subtracted out when evaluating impedance data. In this paper, EIS and distillate conductivity monitoring are used to characterize wetting front progression and pore breakthrough in realtime bench-scale testing for three commonly used commercial, micro­porous, PTFE MD membranes. The precise moment of impedance drop is used to identify the onset of wetting, the slope of the impedance-time curve is used to determine the rate of wetting front progression, and the magnitude of impedance reduction is used to indicate the depth of the wetting front in the membrane pores. Distillate conductivity is used to indicate pore breakthrough. First, reductions in impedance due to compaction are characterized and impedance is normalized to isolate reductions in impedance due to wetting front progression. Then, wetting front progression is characterized under low TMP, to assess the effects of membrane pore size and feed solution concentration for highly soluble NaCl and less-soluble CaSO4 feed solutions. Next, the effect of elevated TMP is assessed at low feed solution concentration. The stability of the wetting front during longer-term (i.e., 14-day) operation is also evalu­ated.

content: TMP is assessed at low feed solution concentration. The stability of the wetting front during longer-term (i.e., 14-day) operation is also evalu­ated. Finally, the surface and cross-sectional morphologies of the membranes before and after operation are characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to understand why and how wetting front progression and transition states occur. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Membranes and membrane characterization Three commercial flat-sheet membranes from Parker Performance Materials (Lee’s Summit, MO) were tested. The QM050 membrane is a single-layer (“unsupported”) PTFE membrane with a nominal pore size of 0.05 μm; the QM022 membrane is a single-layer (unsupported) PTFE membrane with a nominal pore size of 0.2 μm; and the QL822 mem­brane is a composite (“supported”) membrane with a PTFE selective layer that has a nominal pore size of 0.45 μm and a polypropylene support layer. Contact angles on all three membranes were measured using a goniometer (ram´e-hart, Succasunna, NJ) via the sessile drop method with 5-μL droplets of deionized water or feed solutions. The bulk porosity of the membranes was obtained from McGaughey et al. [3], who used the gravimetric method. The surface and cross-sectional morphologies of each membrane before and after operation were char­acterized using a scanning electron microscope (Nova NanoSEM 450, FEI, Hillsboro, OR).

citation.title: Onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression in membrane distillation

content: phologies of each membrane before and after operation were char­acterized using a scanning electron microscope (Nova NanoSEM 450, FEI, Hillsboro, OR). Membrane cross-sections were prepared using a cryo-snap freeze-fracture method from the literature (i.e., [41]). All membrane samples were sputter-coated with Pt/Pd for 40 s before im­aging. Surface micrographs were captured at three arbitrary locations on both sides of the membrane (i.e., on both the feed and distillate sides). ImageJ (version 1.52, National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD) was used to classify lighter and darker areas on the micrographs as mem­brane fibrils and pores, respectively, and to measure the mean surface pore size. Surface porosity was calculated as the ratio of pore area to total membrane surface area.

content: 2.2. Feed solution chemistries Deionized (DI) water was used as the distillate solution in all wetting experiments. ACS-grade NaCl (VWR, Radnor, PA) and CaSO4 (SigmaAldrich, St. Louis, MO) were used to prepare feed solutions. Feed solu­tion concentrations are summarized in Supplementary Information, Tables S1-S3. All initial feed solution concentrations were less than the saturation concentrations of NaCl and CaSO4 at the operating temper­ature of 49 ◦C (i.e., 5.5 M NaCl or 14 mM CaSO4). Because the system was operated in batch mode without replenishment, feed solution con­centration increased over time, but by no more than 0.3 M. Initial and final feed concentrations were calculated based on measured feed con­ductivity using standard curves as described in McGaughey et al. [3]. For cases in which the feed solution conductivity exceeded the maximum limit of the conductivity probe (i.e., 199.9 mS/cm), feed so­lution concentrations were estimated based on distillate flux according to methods described by McGaughey et al. [3]. Briefly, the feed solution concentration was estimated based on the known initial mass of solute in the feed solution and the current mass of water in the feed solution, assuming negligible solute flux and that the mass of water leaving the feed solution was equal to the mass of water passing through the membrane.

content: 2.3. Short-term wetting experiments Membrane samples of size 20 cm2 were installed in a custom acrylic module with commercial spacers (Sterlitech, Kent, WA) on each side of the membrane to provide mechanical support and promote mixing. Rubber gaskets were used in the module to allow room for the spacers in the feed and distillate channels. The module was placed in a bench-scale MD system (Fig. 1) to perform the wetting experiments. The feed stream was circulated through the system using a peristaltic pump (Masterflex, Gelsenkirchen, Germany) and the distillate stream was circulated using a gear pump (Micropump, Vancouver, WA). The feed and distillate streams flowed counter-currently, both with a flow rate of 0.76 L/min. The feed stream was maintained at a temperature of 48 ± 1 ◦C using a recirculating heater (Cole-Parmer, Vernon Hills, IL) and the distillate stream was maintained at 18 ± 1 ◦C using a chiller (Cole-Parmer, Ver­non Hills, IL). A needle valve (McMaster-Carr, Elmhurst, IL) downstream of the feed outlet enabled control of the TMP in the module. Tempera­tures and pressures were monitored at module inlets and outlets using in-line resistance temperature sensors (Omega Engineering, Norwalk, CT) and pressure probes (feed side: Omega Engineering; distillate side: Cole-Parmer). The system was operated in batch mode without replen­ishment of feed solution.

Experimental data were recorded every 30 s using LabView data acquisition software (National Instruments, Austin, TX). 2.4. Longer-term (14-day) wetting experiment For the longer-term (14-day) wetting experiment, a membrane sample of size 140 cm2 was installed in a larger custom acrylic module and the module was placed in a different bench-scale system that was designed for longer-term operation. Details of the module and system can be found in McGaughey et al. [6] and Gustafson et al. [42]. Feed and distillate solutions of 3.42 M (200 g/L) NaCl and DI water were circu­lated at 1.5 L/min. Feed and distillate temperature set points were 65 and 35 ◦C. 2.5. This drop is attributed to decreased mem­brane thickness due to compaction, with a greater instantaneous drop in normalized impedance indicating greater instant compaction [4]. Based on these results, instant compaction is greater in the order QM050>QM022>QL822.

Two titanium electrodes (for the short-term experiments: each 7 * 1 cm; for the long-term experiments: each 12 * 1 cm; SigmaAldrich, St. Louis, MO) were placed in direct contact with each side of the membrane to continuously measure impedance. An operating fre­quency of 100 kHz was selected based on methods developed by Chen et al. [32] and previous testing by Ding et al. [4]. The data in Fig. 2d–f shows representative results for normalized impedance during the wetting experiments. Wetting front progression is described by three parameters: the precise moment of the initial impedance drop indicates the onset of wetting, the slope of the impedance-time curve indicates the rate of wetting front progression, and the magnitude of impedance reduction indicates the depth of the wetting front in the membrane pore(s) (Fig. 3a). Membrane surface and bulk characterization The data in Table 1 show measured characterization results for the membranes alongside data from the literature and from the manufac­turer. The QM050 and QM022 membranes have similar contact angles, indicating similar surface hydrophobicities; the QL822 membrane has a slightly lower contact angle compared to the QM050 and QM022 membranes (by 3%; the difference is statistically significant based on ttests with α = 0.05). The unsupported QM050 and QM022 membranes have similar thicknesses (as confirmed by t-test with α = 0.05), whereas the supported QL822 membrane has greater total thickness. All three membranes have similar surface porosities; the differences in surface porosities between the three membranes are statistically insignificant (as confirmed by t-test with α = 0.05). The QL822 membrane has a larger maximum surface pore size than the QM022 and QM050 mem­branes (by 120 and 140% respectively, which are statistically significant based on t-test with α = 0.05). Based on LEP models, the larger maximum surface pore size is expected to result in lower wetting resistance, making the onset of wetting and pore breakthrough more likely for the QL822 membrane than the QM050 and QM022 mem­branes.

Wetting front progression at low TMP with DI water The data in Fig. 4 show normalized impedance, distillate conduc­tivity, and distillate flux as a function of time for the three membranes with DI water as the feed solution. A low TMP of 2 kPa was maintained in all experiments. Because the feed solution contains no added salt, salt passage will not occur even if pore breakthrough occurs. Thus, in this set of experiments, distillate conductivity is expected to stay close to zero and decreasing normalized impedance would be the only indicator of wetting front progression. For each membrane, normalized impedance decreases only slightly (to ~0.90) during the experiment, indicating that the wetting front re­mains at or near the feed-side surface. The slight reductions in normalized impedance may be due to small decreases in membrane thickness caused by creep. Creep can occur even for low pressures during short-term operation [4]. The average distillate flux for the QM050 membrane (16.5 ± 2 LMH) (Fig. 4a) is slightly less than average distillate flux of the QM022 membrane (20.5 ± 1 LMH) (Fig. 4b), likely due to the smaller nominal pore size of the QM050 membrane compared to the QM022 membrane. Average distillate flux is lowest for the com­posite QL822 membrane (13.3 ± 2 LMH) (Fig. 4c), likely because this is a thicker membrane (Table 1) with greater resistance to mass transfer [3]. 3.3.

The early, rapid, and more substantial reduction in normalized impedance indicates early onset, high rate, and mid-depth progression of the wetting front until it holds its position within the bulk of the membrane. Given that pore break­through occurs in the QL822 membrane when operating with a mod­erate feed solution (1 M NaCl) and at low TMP, the QL822 membrane is not used in subsequent experiments. For all membranes, the depth of the wetting front is greater for the 1 M NaCl feed solution than the 0.6 M NaCl feed solution (comparing Fig. 5a–c with Fig. 5d-f). Wetting front progression from the feed side is likely driven by scaling that reaches greater depths at higher concen­trations. Because distillate flux remained relatively stable at values that were similar to those observed for the DI water feed solutions, scaling did not significantly affect distillate flux. As discussed previously, scaling-induced pore breakthrough for highly soluble salts, including NaCl, was also observed recently by Shi et al. [20]. NaCl scaling was suggested to readily redissolve and recrystallize within membrane pores due to variations in vapor flux and internal concentration polarization – a “self-limiting” wetting effect that eventually resulted in pore break­through [20]. In this study, a similar self-limiting wetting effect is observed but in the absence of pore breakthrough.

As the wetting front progresses deeper into the membrane pore(s), the likelihood of forma­tion of nuclei and salt crystals on pristine membrane fibrils deeper within the membrane pore(s) decreases, as it is more thermodynami­cally favorable for nuclei to form on existing salt crystals (i.e., for sec­ondary crystallization to occur) [50]. Also, the hypothesis that the rate of wetting front progression will be highest through the QL822 mem­brane and lowest through the QM050 membrane based on nominal pore size is generally affirmed by the results in Fig. 5. The role of nominal pore size on pore breakthrough was also shown in a study by Wong et al. [33] who examined the effect of decreasing feed solution surface tension over time; however, our study also shows the role of nominal and/or surface pore size on the depth of wetting front progression in the absence of pore breakthrough. Results thus far show that both higher feed so­lution concentration and larger pore sizes cause the wetting front to progress to greater depths. When both membrane pore size and feed solution concentration are greater, wetting is exacerbated, suggesting that there may be synergistic effects of pore size and feed solution concentration on wetting front progression.

Wetting front progression and stability during longer-term operation Results from 14-day testing of the QM022 membrane with 3.42 M (or 200 g/L) NaCl feed solution are shown in Fig. 8. As can be seen in the inset, the normalized impedance decreases early (in the first hour of operation) and slightly (from 1 to 0.97) as the long-term MD system reaches steady state. In the next hour, normalized impedance decreases rapidly and substantially (to 0.16), indicating that the wetting front progresses into the membrane bulk at a high rate and approaches the distillate side surface of the membrane. From two to four hours of operation, normalized impedance decreases slowly from 0.16 to 0.04. No change in normalized impedance occurs for the remaining days of the experiment. The distillate conductivity increases very slowly and slightly, from an initial value of 1 μS/cm to a value of 16 μS/cm during the first six days of operation. Thereafter, distillate conductivity remains constant and close to zero, confirming that pore breakthrough does not occur. The initial increase in distillate conductivity is attributed to solute mixing, an experimental artifact that can occur in MD systems and is associated with slow mixing of solutes previously present in the system with the DI water added as distillate solution [3].

content: 3.7. Membrane characterization to elucidate wetting resistance mechanisms The data in Table 2 show pore sizes at the distillate-side surface of the QM050 and QM022 membranes before and after operation. Measured surface pore sizes on the feed-side of the membrane are not included because the feed-side surface of the membrane may have been impacted by fouling and/or scaling, which would obscure pore size measure­ments. After all experiments, the used membranes have distillate-side average surface pores that are smaller than those of the pristine mem­brane (Table 2). Reduced pore size is expected to result in greater wetting resistance at the distillate-side surface of the membrane, which may slow or stop wetting front progression, resulting in metastable or stable transition states in which the wetting front is near the distillateside surface of the membrane. The range of average pore size reduc­tion is 10–47%; all else constant, a 10–47% reduction in pore size is expected to increase LEP by ~9–32% according to the Young-Laplace equation. The cross-sectional structures of the QM050 and QM022 membranes were also characterized before and after use for selected experiments and are shown in Fig. 9. The cross-sectional morphologies of the pristine QM050 and QM022 membranes are relatively homogenous with the fibrils that form the porous internal framework appearing uniformly distributed in the membrane cross-section (Fig. 9a and b).

citation.title: Onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression in membrane distillation

content: ely homogenous with the fibrils that form the porous internal framework appearing uniformly distributed in the membrane cross-section (Fig. 9a and b). After use, the cross-sectional membrane morphologies become heterogeneous and stratified, characterized by alternating layers of porous fibril structures and void spaces (Fig. 9c–e) with darker areas representing larger void spaces/pores and lighter areas representing compressed layers with smaller visible pores. Differentiation between the aggregated or com­pressed layers and void spaces are especially pronounced for the QM022 membrane after 14 days of operation with 3.42 M NaCl feed solution (Fig. 9e). The greater differentiation between compressed layers and void spaces may be due to longer-term operation as well as the higher temperature of the feed solution (65 versus 49 ◦C in the shorter exper­iments). Higher operating temperatures have been associated with greater deformation and aggregation of polymer fibrils of PTFE mem­branes (e.g., Ref. [42]).

content: The cross-sectional areas of larger void spaces/pores in Fig. 9c–e may be indicative of significantly lower local wetting resistance that could facilitate wetting front progression towards the distillate side. In addi­tion, large void spaces may cause the sudden jumps in the wetting front that were observed at the higher TMP values (Fig. 7d–f). The crosssectional areas with smaller visible pores (i.e., the compressed layers) may be indicative of higher wetting resistance within the bulk or at the distillate-side surface of the membranes that can slow or stop wetting front progression. The mid-depth and deep transition wetting states observed during MD experiments may be due to the presence of these compressed layers. 4. Conclusions In this study, the onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression in commercial PTFE MD membranes were determined in real-time using EIS and distillate conductivity monitoring. For low and moderate feed solution concentrations, and for TMP values that are well below LEP values, substantial wetting front progression occurs – despite high sur­face hydrophobicity and pore sizes that are typically expected to result in good wetting resistance. Notably, wetting front progression occurs to substantial depths, but in the absence of pore breakthrough.

citation.title: Onset, rate, and depth of wetting front progression in membrane distillation

content: pected to result in good wetting resistance. Notably, wetting front progression occurs to substantial depths, but in the absence of pore breakthrough. Further­more, a longer-term experiment demonstrated that hydrophobic MD membranes can operate sustainably for at least 14 days of continuous operation in a deeply wetted state (i.e., with a very thin air gap). Together, the short-term and longer-term results show that metastable and stable transition states can occur – that commercial PTFE MD membranes can operate not only in a surface-wetted state, but in middepth and deep wetting states, where the wetting front holds its posi­tion in the bulk or near the distillate-side surface of the membrane. Wetting resistance is not an on/off switch: instead, membrane wetting resistance acts as a frictional force, causing wetting fronts to slow down or stop at different depths depending on the membrane pore size, the bulk concentration of the feed solution, and the TMP. For saline solu­tions and at elevated TMPs, while the feed-side surface of the membrane often wets, these results support the hypothesis that the distillate-side surface of the membrane can act as a final barrier to pore break­through, even for very thin air gaps.
